claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. I'll need to ask you some questions to assess your situation.

First, let's start with information about your child (or children):

**How old is your child?** (If you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can check each of them)

- Under 16 years old
- Between 16 and 19 years old
- 20 or older

Please let me know your child's age, and if you have multiple children, please also tell me how many children you're inquiring about.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in finding out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who's 14 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Thank you for that information. Let me process this for Alex who is 14 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news so far! Since Alex is 14 years old (under 16), they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit.

Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

This is a standard check to ensure that children aren't working full-time instead of being in education or under parental care.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all. They're just in school like any other 14-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That's perfectly normal for a 14-year-old.

Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

In other words, is Alex receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in their own name (not as part of your household claim)?

For most 14-year-olds in school, the answer would be that they are NOT receiving these benefits in their own right.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name. They're just a regular schoolkid.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Alex sounds like a typical 14-year-old in school.

One more question about Alex:

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

Most 14-year-olds aren't, but I need to check because certain types of apprenticeships can affect eligibility. For context:
- In England, all apprenticeships disqualify you from Child Benefit
- In Scotland, Modern Apprenticeships disqualify you
- In Wales, most apprenticeships disqualify you (except Foundation Apprenticeships which are approved)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship. They're too young for that - just attending regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Alex meets all the child-related requirements so far.

Now let's check your eligibility as the claimant. 

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources and aren't claiming certain benefits
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** The most straightforward is if you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, no - I don't live in the UK. I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Living abroad doesn't automatically disqualify you, but we need to check if you meet one of the special circumstances.

**Are you living abroad in any of these specific situations?**

1. **Are you a Crown servant** (working for the UK government abroad, such as diplomatic staff, civil servants posted overseas, or members of HM Forces)?

2. **Are you living in a specific qualifying country?** There are certain circumstances where you can claim Child Benefit while living abroad temporarily.

Could you tell me more about your situation? 
- What is your employment status while abroad?
- Are you working for the UK government or armed forces?
- Do you have any connection to the UK (such as paying UK National Insurance, or planning to return)?

This information will help me determine if you meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or working for the UK government. I'm just living abroad - not in any official capacity or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Result

I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** at this time.

### Why You're Not Eligible

While **Alex meets all the requirements as a qualifying child** (being 14 years old, in regular school, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship), **you do not meet the UK residency requirements** as the claimant.

To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- Normally live in the UK, OR
- Meet special circumstances such as:
  - Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with additional qualifying criteria)
  - Being a Crown servant working abroad
  - Living in certain qualifying countries in an official capacity

Since you're living abroad without any of these special circumstances, you cannot claim Child Benefit.

### What You Can Do

- **If you return to the UK**: You would be able to claim Child Benefit once you are normally resident in the UK again
- **If your circumstances change**: If you begin working for the UK government abroad or your residency status changes, you may become eligible

### More Information

For detailed information about residency requirements, visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility

I understand this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any questions or if your circumstances change in the future, please don't hesitate to reach out again.

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old and meets all the child-related requirements: under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad and is not a Crown servant, does not work for the UK government, and does not meet any of the special circumstances that would allow claiming Child Benefit from abroad (such as settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme). Child Benefit requires that the claimant normally lives in the UK or meets one of the specific exceptions, which the claimant does not.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child (Alex, 14 years old) who qualifies as an eligible child for Child Benefit purposes. However, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit because they do not meet the UK residency requirements. They are living abroad without any of the special circumstances that would allow them to claim from overseas.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.
